This expression should be contrasted with the following, which integt function on intervals that approach the singular point $x=0$ in a sy fashion. We define the Cauchy principal value of the integral $\int_{-1}^{1}$ with a singularity at $x=0$, to be (Figure 7)
$$
\text { P.V. } \int_{-1}^{1} f(x) d x=\lim _{r \rightarrow 0^{+}}\left(\int_{-1}^{-r} f(x) d x+\int_{r}^{-1} f(x) d x\right)
$$

The Cauchy principal value of an integral may exist even though the itself is not convergent. For example, $\int_{-1}^{-r} \frac{d x}{x}+\int_{r}^{1} \frac{d x}{x}=0$ for al so P.V. $\int_{-1}^{1} \frac{d x}{x}=0$, but the integral itself is clearly not converg $\int_{0}^{1} \frac{d x}{x}=\infty$.

However, whenever $\int_{-1}^{1} f(x) d x$ is convergent, then P.V. $\int_{-1}^{1} f($ ists, and the two integrals will be the same, since we can split the a sum in (13) into a sum of limits and recover (12). This fact to compute convergent integrals by computing their principal valu will be advantageous when using complex integration.

The extension of principal value integrals to more complicat tions is as follows: To compute the principal value of an integral integrand has possible singularities at $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}$ and the it integration may be infinite in both directions, we take limits to each singularity (and to the infinite interval) one at a time, yet each singularity (or infinity) in a symmetric fashion. If all these lin the principal value exists.
